stage.name: Security & Wallets
stage.description: BIP39/44 seed derivation, wallet types, attack vectors, multisig setups, and XRPL account security.

elements.content: BIP39 Seed Phrase Derivation
From entropy to master private key

128-256 bits
Entropy

2048 words
Wordlist

Entropy to Mnemonic

Generate 128-256 bits of cryptographically secure random entropy

Add checksum: SHA-256(entropy), take first entropy_bits/32 bits

Concatenate entropy + checksum, split into 11-bit groups → index into 2048-word BIP39 wordlist

Result: 12 words (128-bit) or 24 words (256-bit) — each word carries 11 bits of information

Mnemonic to Seed

PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA512(mnemonic phrase, 'mnemonic' + passphrase, 2048 iterations)

Output: 512-bit seed — deterministically generates all child keys

Optional passphrase (25th word): same mnemonic + different passphrase = entirely different wallet

Passphrase enables plausible deniability — 'duress wallet' with small amount vs real wallet

BIP32/44 HD Derivation

Master key: HMAC-SHA512(b'Bitcoin seed', seed) → 512 bits = left(256b private key) + right(256b chain code)

Child key derivation: HMAC-SHA512(parent_pubkey || index) → child private key + chain code

BIP44 path: m/44'/coin_type'/account'/change/address_index — standardized across all wallets

Hardened derivation (index ≥ 2³¹): uses private key, not public key — child keys can't be derived from xpub
Attack Vectors
Phishing, clipboard hijacking, and supply chain attacks

#1 threat
Phishing

$millions lost
Clipboard

Phishing

Fake website: pixel-perfect clone of MetaMask, Ledger, or Uniswap — check URL character by character

Email phishing: 'Your account is compromised, enter seed phrase immediately' — never legitimate

Social engineering: Discord DMs offering 'NFT mint access' or 'beta testing' — leads to drain site

Spear phishing: targeted attack using personal info — researches victim before striking

Clipboard Hijacking

Malware monitors clipboard for cryptocurrency address pattern

When you copy an address, malware silently replaces it with attacker's address

Clipboard trojans have stolen millions — affected Windows, macOS, and mobile devices

Defense: always verify first and last 6 characters of destination address after pasting

Supply Chain Attacks

Fake npm package: 'ethereumjs-wallet' typosquatted as 'ethereumjs-wallets' — steals private keys

Browser extension: malicious extension added to Chrome Web Store reads every page you visit

Hardware wallet fake: counterfeit Ledger with backdoored firmware — buy only from manufacturer

Dependency confusion: inject malicious package with same name as internal private npm package
Multisig Setups
M-of-N security for individuals and organizations

2-of-3
Standard

P2SH, P2WSH
Protocol

How Multisig Works

M-of-N: M signatures required from N possible keys to authorize a transaction

Bitcoin P2SH multisig: redeem script contains N public keys, M required to unlock

Ethereum Gnosis Safe: smart contract wallet — most popular institutional multisig, $100B+ secured

Key diversity: store keys on different devices, locations, even different hardware wallet brands

Common Configurations

2-of-3: one key on laptop, one on hardware wallet, one in secure cold storage — lose one, still recover

3-of-5: for organizations — requires quorum, any 2 keys compromised but not 3 is safe

Time-locked: funds locked for N blocks — limits impact of compromised key

Social recovery (ERC-4337): designate guardians who can collectively reset your key — no seed phrase

Operational Security

Geographic distribution: keys in different cities/countries — natural disaster resilience

Vendor diversity: use keys from different hardware wallet manufacturers — firmware vulnerability can't drain all keys

Test recovery: annually verify you can reconstruct quorum without any assumed key

Key rotation: proactively replace keys on schedule — don't wait for compromise
XRPL Account Security
Trust lines, account deletion, and key management on XRPL

10 XRP
Reserve

3 types
Key types

XRPL Account Structure

Master key pair: generated from seed — ultimate control, used to set other keys

Regular key pair: set via SetRegularKey — rotate without changing account address

Signing key (multisig): SignerListSet transaction enables M-of-N multisig on XRPL

Disable master key: after setting regular key or multisig, disable master key for security

Trust Lines & Reserves

Base reserve: 10 XRP per account (locked, not spendable) — prevents spam account creation

Owner reserve: 2 XRP per trust line, offer, or escrow object — scales with account complexity

Trust line: explicit permission to hold a token (e.g., USD issued by Bitstamp)

Zero-out trust line: set balance to 0 and remove trust line → recover 2 XRP reserve

XRPL-Specific Security

Destination tags: required for exchanges — sending without tag = funds lost to exchange

Check transactions: create a check payable to another account — recipient redeems when ready

Escrow: time-lock or condition-lock XRP — released only after time passes or condition met

Payment channels: off-ledger micropayments with on-ledger settlement — high frequency, low cost
Wallet Types Deep Dive
Hot, cold, hardware, and smart contract wallets

Safest
Hardware

ERC-4337
Smart wallet

Hot Wallets

Browser extension (MetaMask): private key encrypted in browser storage — convenient but exposed to web attacks

Mobile wallet (Trust, Rainbow): key in secure enclave on mobile OS — safer than browser, still connected

Desktop wallet (Exodus, Electrum): key on local file system — safer than mobile if OS is clean

Hot wallets: for daily use, small amounts — not for savings

Cold Storage

Hardware wallet (Ledger, Trezor): key never leaves device, TX signed on-device display

Air-gapped signing: PSBT transferred via QR/USB — signing device never touches internet

Steel backup (Cryptosteel): fire/flood resistant — seed phrase stamped in metal

Paper wallet (deprecated): printed key — degrades, easily photographed, not recommended

Smart Contract Wallets

ERC-4337 (Account Abstraction): wallet = smart contract — enables social recovery, gas abstraction

Bundlers: aggregate multiple ERC-4337 UserOperations into one Ethereum transaction

Paymaster: third party pays gas fees — enables gasless onboarding for new users

Session keys: grant limited signing authority (amount + token + time) to dApps — no need to approve every tx
DeFi Security Practices
Approvals, revoking, and staying safe on-chain

Free tool
Revoke.cash

Unlimited risk
Token approvals

Token Approvals

ERC-20 approve() grants spender address unlimited (or fixed) access to your tokens

Most dApps request unlimited approval — one compromised contract can drain your entire balance

Risky: if Uniswap router contract has a bug, your unlimited approval is exploitable

Best practice: approve exact amount needed for each transaction, not unlimited

Revoking Approvals

Revoke.cash, Etherscan token approvals tab: see all active approvals for your address

Revoke regularly — especially after a protocol is hacked or no longer used

Hardware wallets: confirm each revoke on device — protects against UI spoofing

ENS names: use for readability but verify underlying address hasn't changed before sending

Transaction Simulation

Tenderly, Blocknative: simulate transaction before signing — shows exact token flows

MetaMask snap (Fire): shows human-readable transaction summary with risk warnings

Wallet Guard: browser extension that checks sites against phishing database before you interact

Fake zero-value transfer: attacker sends 0 tokens to poison your history — don't copy addresses from tx history
Operational Security
Devices, networks, and practices for high-value wallets

Strongest
Air-gap

Ongoing
OPSEC

Device Security

Dedicated signing device: old phone or laptop used ONLY for crypto, no apps or browsing

Full disk encryption: FileVault (Mac), BitLocker (Windows), LUKS (Linux)

Firmware updates: keep OS and wallet software updated — most exploits target old versions

No cloud sync: iCloud/Google Drive sync of browser data can expose MetaMask encrypted key

Network Security

VPN: masks IP address from services but doesn't protect against blockchain analysis

Tor: stronger anonymity but slows connections — Tails OS routes all traffic through Tor by default

Public WiFi: never access wallets on public WiFi — man-in-the-middle attacks are trivial

DNS-over-HTTPS: prevents ISP from logging your crypto site visits — enable in browser settings

Privacy Practices

Address reuse: using same address repeatedly links all your transactions — use new address per receive

Coinjoin (Wasabi, JoinMarket): mixes UTXOs with others to break on-chain traceability

Chain analysis: Chainalysis, Elliptic track blockchain forensics for exchanges and governments

Privacy coins (Monero): ring signatures + stealth addresses + confidential transactions by default
